title: Class-Based Views
description:
Django uses Class-Based Views (CBVs) to handle HTTP requests with an object-oriented approach. Each HTTP method (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) maps to a method on the view class. A central `dispatch()` method routes incoming requests to the appropriate handler.

In this problem, you will implement a simplified class-based view system in pure Python.

**Implement the following classes:**

1. **`Request`** - A simple data class with attributes:
   - `method` (str): The HTTP method, e.g., `"GET"`, `"POST"`, `"PUT"`, `"DELETE"`
   - `data` (dict): The request body/payload (default `{}`)
   - `query_params` (dict): URL query parameters (default `{}`)

2. **`Response`** - A simple data class with attributes:
   - `status_code` (int): HTTP status code
   - `data` (any): The response body

3. **`View`** (base class):
   - `dispatch(request)` method: Looks up the method name (lowercased, e.g., `"get"`, `"post"`) on `self`. If the handler exists, call it with `request` and return the result. If not, return a `Response(405, {"error": "Method not allowed"})`.
   - `as_view()` class method: Returns a callable (function) that creates an instance of the class and calls `dispatch` on it.

4. **`ListView(View)`**:
   - Has a class attribute `items = []` (to be overridden by subclasses).
   - `get(request)`: Returns `Response(200, self.items)`.

5. **`CreateView(View)`**:
   - Has a class attribute `items = []` and `required_fields = []`.
   - `post(request)`: Validates that all `required_fields` are present in `request.data`. If any are missing, return `Response(400, {"error": "Missing fields", "fields": <sorted list of missing fields>})`. Otherwise, append `request.data` to `self.items` and return `Response(201, request.data)`.

6. **`DetailView(View)`**:
   - Has a class attribute `items = []` and `lookup_field = "id"`.
   - `get(request)`: Finds the first item in `self.items` where `item[self.lookup_field]` equals `request.query_params[self.lookup_field]`. Returns `Response(200, item)` if found, or `Response(404, {"error": "Not found"})` if not.

examples.input:
request = Request(method="GET")
view = View()
response = view.dispatch(request)
(response.status_code, response.data)
examples.explanation: The base View has no get() method, so dispatch returns 405.

examples.input:
class BookList(ListView):
    items = [{"id": 1, "title": "Django Unleashed"}]

request = Request(method="GET")
view = BookList()
response = view.dispatch(request)
(response.status_code, response.data)
examples.explanation: ListView.get() returns all items with status 200.

constraints: Request.method is always an uppercase string: "GET", "POST", "PUT", or "DELETE"
dispatch() must convert method to lowercase to find the handler
as_view() returns a callable that accepts a Request and returns a Response
CreateView must validate all required_fields are present in request.data
Missing fields list in the error response must be sorted alphabetically
DetailView looks up items using the lookup_field key in query_params

test_cases.input:
class BookCreate(CreateView):
    items = []
    required_fields = ["title", "author"]
r = BookCreate().dispatch(Request(method='POST', data={"title": "DRF Guide", "author": "Tom"}))
(r.status_code, r.data)

class BookCreate(CreateView):
    items = []
    required_fields = ["title", "author"]
r = BookCreate().dispatch(Request(method='POST', data={"title": "Incomplete"}))
(r.status_code, r.data)

class BookDetail(DetailView):
    items = [{"id": 1, "title": "Django"}, {"id": 2, "title": "Flask"}]
    lookup_field = "id"
r = BookDetail().dispatch(Request(method='GET', query_params={"id": 2}))
(r.status_code, r.data)

class BookDetail(DetailView):
    items = [{"id": 1, "title": "Django"}]
    lookup_field = "id"
r = BookDetail().dispatch(Request(method='GET', query_params={"id": 99}))
(r.status_code, r.data)
